topic: Design-based Wald inference for ego-cluster network experiments via exposure-incidence polynomials: finite-population HT exposure contrasts, closed first/joint exposure probabilities, and a dependency-graph CLT under bounded ego-overlap

open_problems.open_problem: Derive closed first- and joint-exposure probabilities for ego-cluster HT contrasts when exposure events are shared ego-neighborhood incidence polynomials rather than independent cellwise exposure kernels.

source_refs.claim: Frames interference analysis as design plus exposure mapping plus estimands, with IPW estimators requiring exposure probabilities and joint exposure probabilities.
source_refs.author: Peter M. Aronow and Cyrus Samii

source_refs.claim: Shows graph-cluster randomization admits efficient exact algorithms for selected network-exposure probabilities, but not a general ego-incidence polynomial calculus.
source_refs.author: Johan Ugander, Brian Karrer, Lars Backstrom, and Jon Kleinberg

source_refs.claim: Develops ego-cluster randomization with consistency and asymptotic normality under a model-based framework, leaving design-based exposure-probability algebra open.
source_refs.author: Xiao Liu, Feifang Hu, and Jingfei Zhang

source_refs.excerpt: every strict gap ... has a finite certificate supported on the capacitated incidence system
start with K_i=1 matching/cover certificates before claiming the fully capacitated b-matching case

open_problems.why_unsolved: The web literature gives the HT/IPW estimand and variance target but either permits brute-force arbitrary exposure enumeration or supplies exact algorithms only for selected graph-cluster exposure conditions. The prior proposal identified the same combinatorial obstruction from the repository side: multiple unit exposure events must be realized by one shared ego/edge incidence system, and the reviewer explicitly narrowed the unresolved certificate problem to simpler matching/cover cases before the fully capacitated case.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A closed formula or polynomial-time algebraic algorithm for pi_i(e) and pi_ij(e,e') for ego-cluster incidence-polynomial exposure events, together with finite feasibility inequalities or certificates for the shared ego-incidence system.

writing_exemplar.why: It is the cleanest motivation template for moving from interference to exposure mappings, design probabilities, HT contrasts, and variance.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: general interference -> exposure mappings make estimands operational -> HT/Wald inference needs first and joint exposure probabilities -> ego-cluster incidence polynomials need closed probability algebra

method_exemplars.technique: Exact graph-cluster exposure-probability computation and HT weighting, including the proof that selected network-exposure probabilities are computable without full assignment enumeration.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It solves the closest probability-computation problem, but for selected network exposure conditions rather than arbitrary ego-cluster incidence polynomials.

method_exemplars.technique: Randomization-based HT exposure contrasts and variance formulas built from first and joint exposure probabilities.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The proposed closed formulas would plug directly into this estimator-and-variance architecture.

open_problems.open_problem: Construct a finite ego-network experiment where each marginal exposure propensity is locally feasible but the joint exposure endpoint is impossible under one shared completion.

source_refs.excerpt: The obstruction is joint: the same hidden edge must simultaneously determine R_i(G,A) and R_j(G,A)
Add the four-unit capacity witness as an explicit numerical corollary or example

open_problems.why_unsolved: The prior proposal claimed strict shared-vs-cellwise nonsharpness but left the concrete small witness and endpoint arithmetic unproved; the reviewer treated the four-unit capacity witness as the next needed strengthening, not as an already delivered result.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A fully enumerated four-unit or small ego-cluster design with assignment law, exposure events, feasible edge vectors, and a strict shared-completion versus cellwise endpoint inequality.

writing_exemplar.why: Its exposure-mapping formulation gives the right language for explaining why marginal exposure probabilities are not enough for joint design-based inference.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: known interference can be encoded by exposures -> HT estimands use exposure probabilities -> joint exposure events can impose shared feasibility constraints -> a finite witness should separate marginal feasibility from joint realizability

method_exemplars.technique: Explicit enumeration of network exposure conditions under graph-cluster assignment and comparison of feasible exposure events.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The proposed witness is a finite network-exposure feasibility calculation, structurally closest to the exposure-event enumeration in graph-cluster randomization.

method_exemplars.technique: Design-based variance dependence on joint exposure probabilities.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The finite witness would matter because joint exposure probabilities are exactly the quantities driving HT variance and Wald studentization.

open_problems.open_problem: Find a non-definitional decision procedure or minimal feature basis that separates count-aliased exposure laws over a named ego-graph class.

source_refs.excerpt: Reuse the C6/2K3 table only as a sanity-check witness
Generalize the graph class beyond two-regular aliases and give an explicit decision procedure

open_problems.why_unsolved: The banked C6 versus two-triangles alias was only a sanity-check witness, while the attempted frontier collapsed to a quotient definition rather than a theorem that identifies which rooted local features actually separate exposure laws.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A decision procedure or minimal-basis theorem for exposure-incidence polynomials over a specified ego-graph class, with a proof that the basis separates exactly the non-aliased exposure laws.

writing_exemplar.why: It motivates why graph structure and exposure definitions jointly determine whether an experiment can identify network exposure effects.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: social interference breaks ordinary A/B tests -> graph exposure conditions encode interference -> exact exposure probabilities depend on graph structure -> count-aliased ego laws require separating incidence features

method_exemplars.technique: Graph-theoretic exposure-condition characterization and exact exposure-probability algorithms.
method_exemplars.why_closest: A separation basis for ego exposure laws would be the structural analogue of characterizing which graph exposure events are computable and distinguishable.

open_problems.open_problem: Prove a design-based dependency-graph CLT for finite-population HT or Hajek exposure contrasts whose neighborhoods are explicit functions of bounded ego-overlap and exposure-polynomial degree.

source_refs.claim: Establishes consistency and asymptotic normality for exposure-mapping estimators under local-dependence assumptions.
source_refs.author: Peter M. Aronow and Cyrus Samii

source_refs.claim: Uses Stein's method to prove asymptotic normality under a restricted interference condition limiting dependency-graph degree.
source_refs.author: Alex Chin

source_refs.claim: Proves IPW estimators are approximately normal under approximate neighborhood interference, topology restrictions, and growing-network asymptotics.
source_refs.author: Michael P. Leung

open_problems.why_unsolved: Existing CLTs are stated for broad local-dependence graphs, approximate neighborhood interference, or standard no-interference estimators under restricted dependency degree. They do not identify the dependency graph generated by overlapping ego clusters and incidence-polynomial exposure events, so the needed normal approximation is not yet expressed in terms of ego-overlap counts, cluster size, and polynomial degree.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A Wald-ready finite-population CLT with variance consistency conditions and dependency-neighborhood bounds stated as explicit functions of maximum ego-overlap, maximum ego-cluster size, and exposure-polynomial degree.

writing_exemplar.why: It frames the move from restrictive local-interference assumptions to usable asymptotic inference under graph-topology conditions.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: local interference is too sharp -> approximate/network dependence conditions preserve causal exposure targets -> asymptotic normality enables Wald inference -> ego-overlap should yield primitive dependency neighborhoods

method_exemplars.technique: Stein-method dependency-graph CLT for randomized experiments under interference, with proof terms controlled by maximum dependency degree.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The bounded-ego-overlap theorem would specialize this dependency-graph technology to the explicit graph induced by ego-cluster exposure polynomials.

method_exemplars.technique: Exposure-mapping HT estimators and local-dependence asymptotic normality.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It is the direct estimator target whose local-dependence assumptions need to be replaced by bounded ego-overlap primitives.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize when exact or sharp conservative finite-population Wald variance estimators exist for ego-cluster exposure contrasts with computable nonzero joint exposure probabilities.

source_refs.claim: Shows a finite-population network HAC variance estimator is biased, with the bias interpretable as variance of unit-level exposure effects.
source_refs.author: Michael P. Leung

source_refs.claim: Finds regression-based network-robust standard errors can be anti-conservative and proposes an adjusted covariance estimator.
source_refs.author: Mengsi Gao and Peng Ding

source_refs.claim: Provides randomization-based variance estimators accounting for complex clustering under interference.
source_refs.author: Peter M. Aronow and Cyrus Samii

open_problems.why_unsolved: Computing joint exposure probabilities only solves the design-probability side of the variance problem. The finite-population variance also contains cross-products of potential outcomes under exposure pairs that are not jointly observable, and recent network-regression work shows that convenient robust covariance estimators can be anti-conservative.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A theorem giving either an exactly unbiased variance estimator under stated additive or constant exposure-effect restrictions, or a sharp Neyman-style conservative upper bound for HT/Hajek Wald exposure contrasts under ego-cluster designs.

writing_exemplar.why: It motivates the inference gap through a concrete failure mode: standard network-robust standard errors can be anti-conservative.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: network experiments need regression/Wald analysis -> robust standard errors can fail under interference -> adjusted covariance restores coverage -> ego-cluster exposure contrasts need exact or sharp conservative variance theory

method_exemplars.technique: Network HAC variance construction and finite-population bias decomposition as unit-level exposure-effect variation.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It directly addresses the exact-versus-conservative variance tension under interference.

method_exemplars.technique: Hajek/WLS equivalence and adjusted network-robust covariance proof for design-based network experiments.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It provides the closest Wald-style covariance machinery for exposure estimators.

open_problems.open_problem: Design ego-cluster randomization schemes that optimize finite-population positivity, dependency degree, and Wald precision for exposure-polynomial HT/Hajek contrasts.

source_refs.claim: Introduces an ego-clustering algorithm that sequentially selects egos and assigns alters to minimize asymptotic variances under a model-based framework.
source_refs.author: Xiao Liu, Feifang Hu, and Jingfei Zhang

source_refs.claim: Derives convergence rates for spatial cluster-randomized designs and obtains near-optimal estimator-design pairs under minimal interference assumptions.
source_refs.author: Michael P. Leung

source_refs.claim: Randomized graph-cluster randomization reduces HT/Hajek variance and gives polynomial rather than exponential variance bounds under metric assumptions.
source_refs.author: Johan Ugander and Hao Yin

open_problems.why_unsolved: Existing design work optimizes graph or spatial clustering, or optimizes ego-cluster estimators under a model-based framework. It does not optimize the design-based objective created jointly by exposure-polynomial positivity, first and joint exposure probabilities, dependency-neighborhood size, and finite-population Wald variance.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A design criterion and approximation guarantee for choosing ego clusters that controls minimum exposure probability, maximum dependency-neighborhood size, and an estimable upper bound on HT/Hajek Wald variance.

writing_exemplar.why: It connects a design modification directly to HT/Hajek variance behavior, which is the desired narrative arc here.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: fixed graph clustering can be unlucky -> randomize cluster construction -> improve HT/Hajek variance -> ego-cluster designs should optimize positivity, dependency, and Wald precision

method_exemplars.technique: Ego-cluster construction and variance-guided ego selection under network spillovers.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It is the closest ego-cluster design paper, but its target is model-based rather than finite-population exposure-polynomial Wald inference.

method_exemplars.technique: Rate-optimal cluster-randomized design analysis under decaying spatial interference.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It supplies a design-rate proof template that could be adapted from spatial clusters to ego-overlap and exposure-polynomial dependency.

literature_map:
Hudgens and Halloran (2008) supply the foundational direct/indirect/total/overall effects language under interference.
Aronow and Samii (2017) turn general interference into a design-based exposure-mapping/IPW program, but exposure and joint-exposure probabilities remain the bottleneck.
Ugander, Karrer, Backstrom, and Kleinberg (2013) show exact network-exposure probabilities for graph-cluster randomization; Eckles, Karrer, and Ugander (2017) emphasize bias reduction through clustered assignment and exposure-aware analysis.
Athey, Eckles, and Imbens (2018), Basse, Feller, and Toulis (2019), and Puelz et al. (2022) focus on exact/conditional randomization tests when interference nulls are non-sharp.
Savje, Aronow, and Hudgens (2021) show robustness under unknown limited interference for generalized average effects, while warning that conventional confidence statements can fail.
Leung's approximate-neighborhood and rate-optimal cluster work moves the field toward asymptotic normality, network HAC, and design-rate tradeoffs under weaker local-interference assumptions.
Chin (2018) gives Stein-method CLTs using dependency graphs, suggesting a route to bounded ego-overlap asymptotics.
Gao and Ding (2023) connect Hajek exposure estimators to weighted least squares and show network-robust standard errors may be anti-conservative.
Forastiere, Mealli, and Airoldi extend exposure/spillover identification to observational network settings via generalized propensity scores, highlighting treatment-neighborhood confounding rather than randomized design probability computation.
Goldsmith-Pinkham and Imbens, Bramoulle-Djebbari-Fortin, and Manski are useful econometric anchors for peer-effect identification and reflection, but the current cluster is randomized design-based inference rather than structural peer effects.
Karwa and Airoldi (2023) question the optimality/admissibility of HT estimators under network interference, sharpening the HT-versus-alternative-estimator tension.
Thiyageswaran, McCormick, and Brennan (2024) make exposure-threshold misspecification explicit, motivating sensitivity or adaptive exposure choices.
Liu, Hu, and Zhang (2026) are the closest ego-cluster target, but their contribution is model-based; the open design-based gap is exact exposure probabilities, finite-population variance, and Wald CLTs under bounded ego-overlap.
prior_proposal_map:
Inspected target exp_ego_cluster_exposure_clt: only state.json/pipeline.jsonl exist, no authored proposal yet.
Closest positive prior is pid_network_exposure_v1: shared-completion ADE LP, unproved strict cellwise nonsharpness, and unproved capacitated-incidence certificates.
Adjacent failed network_random_set and network_iv tracks mainly warn that finite witnesses must be fully enumerated and feasible; their broken witnesses were not promoted.
pid_network_exposure_aliasing_graphpair_v1 supplies a correct C6/2K3 exposure-alias sanity witness but no generic frontier.
exp_saturation_skew_threshold_v1 supplies finite-population randomization-design residue: heterogeneity needed for nonzero skew coefficients and exact finite-M rounding.
panel_approx_nbhd_interference_v1 is a negative inference lesson: avoid tautological stacked-operator CLT/equivalence frontiers; derive primitive conditions instead.
SUBSTRATE_DEBT records the local-dependence CLT/Wald coverage gate as resolved, so it was not listed as an open problem.
